coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor based molecularly imprinted nanoparticles for visual detection of okadaic acid in seawater and sediment
coredata.dc:creator: Lian, Ziru
Zhao, Min
Wang, Jiangtao
Yu, Ren-Cheng
coredata.dc:description: A dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor based on novel molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which possesses self-referencing ability by nitrobenzoxadiazole (NBD) as recognition signal and new carbon dots (CDs) as interior label, has been prepared for sensitive and visual detection of okadaic acid (OA) in seawater and sediment samples. The analyte can rapidly enhance the fluorescence intensity of NBD at 549 nm, but will not change the CDs’ fluorescence signal at 667 nm. The nanosensor has a linear fluorescence enhancement response in the range of 100∼1770 ng L−1 with a limit of detection of 25 ng L−1, and excellent selective recognition for OA over its interferences. The ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor has been successfully applied to detect OA in seawater and sediment samples. The results show that the nanosensor has high recoveries at four spiking levels of OA ranged from 90.0 % to 107.0 % with the relative standard deviation less than 4.1 % (n = 3). More importantly, a reliable visual sense change with two distinct color tonality from bright pink to yellow green can be easily differentiated by the naked eyes during fluorescence analysis, which demonstrates promising potential for the applications of molecularly imprinted sensors in trace analysis of marine environmental matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Okadaic acid
Seawater and sediment
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Visual detection
Surface imprinting
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and recognition mechanism of MIPs sensor
Effect of material concentration and stability evaluation
Influence of pH and ionic strength
Analytical performance of MIPs sensor for OA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. ZR2019MD026 ), Open Fund of CAS Key Laboratory of Marine Ecology and Environmental Sciences, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. KLMEES202003 ) and the Joint Funds of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. U1806202 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A-UV absorption spectra of NBD-Cl (a), APTES-NBD (b), and fluorescence emission spectra of APTES-NBD (c). B-Fluorescence emission spectra of green emissive APTES-NBD (a), red emissive CDs@SiO2 (b) and MIPs (c) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

A-Effects of the addition of MIPs on detection system (n = 3). Curve a: the relative variation rate of fluorescence intensity of detection system (containing MIPs and 160 ng L−1 of OA) vs. addition of MIPs; Curve b: the relative fluorescence intensity vs. addition of only MIPs. B-The fluorescence stability of MIPs. Statistical analysis of the data was performed using SPSS 25.0 (IBM Corp., USA) with one-way variance analysis (ANOVA) followed by Bonferroni statistical tests. Differences were considered significant when p ≤ 0.05. Fg represents the fluorescence intensity of green signal, and Fr represents the fluorescence intensity of red signal (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

A-The effects of different pH on the ratio of fluorescence intensity of MIPs with a concentration of 0.4 mg mL−1 (n = 3). B-The effects of different pH on the ratio of fluorescence detection of MIPs in the presence of OA. Experiment condition: MIPs, 0.4 mg mL-1; OA, 320 ng L−1 (n = 3). C-The effects of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of MIPs for OA. Experiment condition: MIPs, 0.4 mg mL-1; OA, 160 ng L−1. Differences were considered significant when p ≤ 0.05. D-Fluorescence response time of MIPs for OA (n = 3). Experiment condition: MIPs, 0.4 mg mL-1; OA, 160 ng L−1.

A-The fluorescence spectra and corresponding photographs of MIPs upon the exposure to different concentrations of OA. B-The linear relationships of MIPs upon the exposure to different concentrations of OA (n = 3). C-Fluorescence spectra, linear relationships (n = 3) and the corresponding photographs of the SiO2@NBD@MIPs system upon the exposure to different concentrations of OA. D-Fluorescence spectra, linear relationships (n = 3) and the corresponding photographs of the NIPs system upon the exposure to different concentrations of OA.
Schematic illustration for synthetic procedure of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs nanosensor and recognition mechanism for OA.
Recovery and RSD of OA from spiked seawater samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All the results represent mean values of three measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery and RSD of OA from spiked sediment samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All the results represent mean values of three measurements.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor based molecularly imprinted nanoparticles for visual detection of okadaic acid in seawater and sediment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marine College, Shandong University, Weihai 264209, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Marine College, Shandong University, Weihai 264209, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Marine Ecology and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Marine Ecology and Environmental Sciences, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Qingdao 266071, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor based on novel molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which possesses self-referencing ability by nitrobenzoxadiazole (NBD) as recognition signal and new carbon dots (CDs) as interior label, has been prepared for sensitive and visual detection of okadaic acid (OA) in seawater and sediment samples. The analyte can rapidly enhance the fluorescence intensity of NBD at 549 nm, but will not change the CDs’ fluorescence signal at 667 nm. The nanosensor has a linear fluorescence enhancement response in the range of 100∼1770 ng L−1 with a limit of detection of 25 ng L−1, and excellent selective recognition for OA over its interferences. The ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor has been successfully applied to detect OA in seawater and sediment samples. The results show that the nanosensor has high recoveries at four spiking levels of OA ranged from 90.0 % to 107.0 % with the relative standard deviation less than 4.1 % (n = 3). More importantly, a reliable visual sense change with two distinct color tonality from bright pink to yellow green can be easily differentiated by the naked eyes during fluorescence analysis, which demonstrates promising potential for the applications of molecularly imprinted sensors in trace analysis of marine environmental matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Harmful algal blooms (HABs) formed by the proliferation of specific groups of microalgae have negative impacts on aquatic ecosystems, fisheries, tourism and public health [1]. Marine biotoxins produced by some toxic HAB-forming microalgae comprise various groups of naturally occurring compounds, which can be either hydrophilic or lipophilic. Among them, lipophilic toxins in seawater and sediment have low polarity and high stability [2,3]. Okadaic acid (OA), a lipophilic toxin with low molecular weight, is produced by toxic dinoflagellate species in genera Dinophysis and Prorocentrum [4]. Although OA and its derivatives named dinophysistoxins (DTXs) have no harmful effect to shellfish, they are the major agents leading to diarrhetic shellfish poisoning (DSP) in humans [5]. Currently, national monitoring programs of marine biotoxins in commercial bivalves have been routinely operated in many countries, including the member countries of the European Union, the US and Canada. Based on the toxicity to humans, the European Commission established a maximum permitted level of 160 μg of OA equivalent kg−1 shellfish meat in live bivalve molluscs [6]. The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has restricted action levels for marine biotoxins, which is about 0.16 ppm for OA [7], and the regulation is only available for shellfish. It should be noted that, however, no official criteria are currently available for biotoxins in marine environmental samples. Due to the stability of OA in seawater and sediment [8,9], the biotoxin in marine environment is undoubtedly a potential danger to both aquaculture and human health. Thus to examine the occurrence of OA in seawater and marine sediment could be a priori, and it is necessary to have a rapid and sensitive approach to quantify its presence in environmental samples.

As a desirable optical approach, fluorescence technique provides a convenient sensitive detection platform, and has been widely utilized as fluorescence sensors. The technique has remarkable advantages in the analysis of trace substances because of its simplicity, high sensitivity and cost-effective instrumentation [10]. Carbon dots (CDs) derived from carbon materials are the newest member of the long-known semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) series, and have attracted tremendous attention in recent years. The CDs have many features exceeding characteristic of conventional QDs, including excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, bright photoluminescence in the visible spectrum, tunable surface functionalities, low toxicity, high stability and simple synthetic routes [11]. The unique and attractive merits make them appropriate for applications in analytical sensors after surface modifications [12]. Nevertheless, selectivity to targets is still a challenging concern for CDs-based sensors. It is hard to detect the analyte in complex backgrounds due to the overlap of signals between autofluorescence property of some interferential substances and the fluorescence emission peak of CDs, and they are easy to be quenched by interferents [13]. Surface modifications of CDs combined with molecular imprinting technique have a promising potential to improve the selectivity, sensitivity, and resistant ability to interference.

Molecular imprinting allows the preparation of tailor-made synthetic biomimetic materials, namely molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which possess specific binding sites complimentary to the shape, size, and alignment of functional groups suitable for pre-determined target molecules or structurally related derivatives [14]. As plastic artificial receptors, the MIPs with antibody-like recognition abilities exhibit excellent specificity, remarkable chemical/mechanical stability, fast equilibrium kinetics and good reusability [15]. Specifically, MIPs-coated QDs materials congregate the benefits of MIPs and QDs and thus represent significant changes and ultra-sensitive fluorescent properties. In comparison with other light-based methods, MIPs-based fluorescence sensors have been widely brought to the front of the optical sensors [16]. However, the fluorescence response may be interfered by some analyte-independent factors including the background fluorescence signals, the concentration of fluorescent materials, and environmental conditions [17]. To address these obstacles, the ratiometric fluorescence measurement can provide not only an intrinsic correction for eliminating unfavorable effects due to their relative change in uncalibrated signal ratios, but also distinct color changes observed by the naked eyes. Particularly, the visual detection can effectively avoid expensive instrumentation and imaging setups, that holds great promise in MIPs-based fluorescence sensing fields [18].

In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence enhancement sensor based on a novel MIPs-coated CDs nanocomposites has been proposed with a dual-emitting turn off-on switch system for sensitive and visual detection of OA. The eco-friendly CDs obtained by hydrothermal treatment of plant leaves were employed as interior label, and the conjugates of 3-amino-propyltriethoxysilane and nitrobenzoxadiazole (APTES-NBD) played the role of signal recognition unit. The imprinted polymeric shell for OA was formed around the CDs@SiO2 by copolymerization method and then the characterization and binding properties of the MIPs were systematically investigated. The MIPs nanosensor can selectively rebind the analyte with the addition of OA and the fluorescence intensity of APTES-NBD will rapidly be enhanced along with the fluorescence bicolor changes, eventually to fulfill the visual detection of OA. The synthesis of nanosensor and fluorescent detection of OA was depicted in Scheme 1 . The key advance of the current research involves the actual application of the novel ratiometric fluorescent MIPs-based nanosensor for sensitive detection of OA from complex seawater and sediment samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and recognition mechanism of MIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Innovative imprinted composite materials are crucial to build MIPs-based sensors and the whole construction procedure of the dual-emission fluorescent sensor for OA detection are illustrated in Scheme 1. Firstly, the red CDs from Cercis gigantea leaves were extracted in ethanol solvent with an emission peak at approximately 667 nm, and then incorporated into silica nanoparticles via the sol-gel method to obtain CDs@silica composites as interior label material. The silica coating around the CDs can hamper direct contact with the external solution to provide a reliable correction signal during the analysis process. Then the imprinted polymeric shell for OA recognition was created around the CDs@ silica by copolymerization, employing the monensin sodium salt (MSS, structural formula shown in Fig. S1) as dummy template, APTES as functional monomer, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as crosslinker and the conjugating APTES-NBD as a detection signal. After the removal of MSS, the resultant ratiometric MIPs sensor exhibited well-resolved dual emissions at 549 nm and 667 nm by a 420 nm single excitation.

The possible recognition mechanism of MIPs sensor was concluded that a charge transfer could happen between the electron-rich amino group of APTES and the electron-deficient aromatic ring of NBD when they simultaneously existed on the surface of imprinted shell, and then the nitrogen atom of the free primary amine would result in the fluorescence quenching of NBD partially by a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. With the appearance of OA in the analysis system, the hydrogen bond between amino and carboxyl group was formed and the electron-donating ability of nitrogen atoms was effectively decreased in the presence of the resultant positively charged amine. Thus, the PET pathway would be switched off and the fluorescence intensity at 549 nm was significantly enhanced and the MIPs had distinct dual-emission peaks at 549 nm and 667 nm, respectively. From the inserted photographs in Scheme 1, the intrinsic CDs, OA-MIPs and MIPs without the introduction of OA showed the fluorescence changes of red, yellow green and bright pink in color tonality.

The surface morphologies and size of the synthesized products were profiled by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss, Germany) in Fig.S2. It can be clearly observed that MIPs and the non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) exhibited a nanospherical shape with an average diameter of about 20∼30 nm and the imprinting shell thickness was uniform (Fig. S2-A,B). The roughened and uneven surface on the MIPs was displayed from the SEM image (Fig.S2-C), which indicated that there were the imprinting cavities left behind in MIPs due to the presence of the dummy templates in the process of polymerization. These cavities were covered on the surface or inside of MIPs, that would lead to the fluorescence enhancement in the adsorption process of MIPs towards the analyte. Besides, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of the CDs@silica (a), MIPs (b) and NIPs (c) are respectively shown in Fig. S2-D, and the data analysis was explicated in Supplementary Material. The size distribution measurements were also performed on Nano-ZS Zetasizer (Malvern, UK) instrument and shown in Fig. S3. The size distribution of MIPs particles was in the range from 120 to 190 nm with a dominant distribution peak around 164 nm. The average size of 141 nm was larger than that from the TEM image due to slight aggregation and adhesion as observed in TEM and SEM. Furthermore, there was no significant change in particles diameters during the measurements of three days, suggesting no apparent aggregation.

As observed by the UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 1 -A, the absorption peak of monomer NBD-Cl was at 334 nm (curve a) while APTES-NBD increased an absorption peak at 468 nm (curve b). The increased absorption peak was resulted from the substitution of Cl atom on NBD-Cl atom by nitrogen atom, which was associated with the fluorescence of NBD. The fluorescence emission peak of APTES-NBD was at 549 nm in Fig. 1-A-c, which indicates that the fluorescence can only be emitted after coupling with the reagent containing free amino group. The combination of UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum clearly suggested that the NBD be indeed modified on silica and the fluorescent APTES-NBD conjugates be successfully prepared, in consonance with the data of FT-IR spectra.

The fluorescence spectra of APTES-NBD, CDs@SiO2 and MIPs are exhibited in Fig. 1-B. The fluorescence emission peak of APTES-NBD is at 549 nm (curve a), and the emission peak of CDs@SiO2 is at 667 nm (curve b). When the two different fluorescence substances were simultaneously embedded in the same nanocomposite, the resulting MIPs possessed two emitting peaks at 549 and 667 nm and retained the fluorescent properties of two materials (curve c). Meanwhile, the two emission peaks were far apart with two distinct color tonalities by a single wavelength excitation of 420 nm. This result also confirms the successful preparation of the ingenious ratiometric fluorescent imprinted nanosensor.

To achieve a reliable signal response and high sensitivity, the influence of several important factors which may affect the recognition performance of MIPs sensor, was investigated including the concentration of fluorescent nanocomposites, the effect of solvent pH and ionic strength, and response time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of material concentration and stability evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the concentration of fluorescent substances has a remarkable influence on the quenching efficiency, so the amount of MIPs on the OA detection system was first studied. The effect of material concentration was assessed by the (Fg-Fg0)/Fg0, which can reflect the detection sensitivity and the linear range from higher fluorescence intensity of MIPs. Herein, Fg represents the fluorescence intensity of green signal, and Fr represents the fluorescence intensity of red signal. Linear range and sensitivity are two important indicators in the process of fluorescence detection. If the concentration of fluorescent substances is too high, the detection sensitivity for the analyte will be reduced. Conversely, if the concentration of fluorescent substances in the detection system is too low, the linear range of the target will be relatively narrow [19]. Hence, various amounts of imprinted nanoparticles were investigated in detail and the result is shown in Fig. 2 -A. With the lower concentration of MIPs (such as 0.1, 0.2 or 0.3 mg mL−1), a little mount of OA could bring significant variation of fluorescence intensity with high sensitivity, while a narrow linear range was also revealed at the same time. Likewise, fluorescence intensity would be enhanced with the addition of MIPs amount, while the enhancing efficiency was gradually inconspicuous. The experimental results show that 0.4 mg mL−1 of MIPs is the optimal material concentration for OA detection so as to gain high sensitivity and wide linear range.

In addition, the fluorescence stability of MIPs was investigated by illuminating them with UV light using a UV lamp of 365 nm and conducting consecutive measurements of the fluorescence signal over time. The relative fluorescence intensity of the MIPs solution had no significant changes for at least 10 days exposure at ambient temperature (Fig.2-B), which indicated that the prepared fluorescence imprinted material can disperse evenly in the solution without apparent sedimentation or agglomeration and have preferable stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH and ionic strength
ce:section.ce:para: The acidity of the detection system is one of important factors that can affect not only the charge of the binding sites on MIPs, but also the charge of OA. Therefore, the solvent pH was tested in the absence and the presence of OA under different pH conditions from 4.0 to 10.0 by adding Britton-Robinson buffer solution. On the one hand, the change of pH had an inapparent effect on the relative fluorescence intensity of MIPs at intervals, which indicates that the existence of the polymer layer can well protect the internal reference CDs (Fig. 3 -A). On the other hand, the MIPs mixed with OA were also detected over the range of 4.0–10.0, and the enhancing efficiency of the detection system achieved the optimum value at 6.0 (Fig.3-B). Considering the two influence factors of both fluorescence intensity and adsorption properties, the pH 6.0 was optimal for all experiments and the Britton-Robinson buffer was used to adjust the pH of detection system.

It will be definitely a major challenge for seawater samples due to high-salt contents, high ionic strength, and/or complex matrices. Thus, it is necessary to investigate the influence of ionic strength on adsorption properties in this experiment. Various NaCl concentrations as a strong electrolyte were employed to test the effect of ionic strength on the enhancing efficiency of MIPs (Fig. 3-C). Statistical analysis of the data was performed using SPSS 25.0 (IBM Corp., USA) with one-way variance analysis (ANOVA) followed by Bonferroni statistical tests. The result implies that little influence of ionic strength on the enhancing efficiency had been found, even under the condition of high-salt concentration.

To ensure the completion of sensor response between OA and the recognition sites of MIPs, the OA standard solution with a known concentration was added to MIPs test solution and the response time was recorded every 2.0 min by the repeated detections of the fluorescence intensity. The result indicates that the relative fluorescence intensity of the test solution increased rapidly within 10 min and then kept almost unchanged (Fig. 3-D). Hence, signal readout was conducted after 10 min and the rapid response will undoubtedly be a promising application in the field monitoring of marine biotoxins. It is concluded that the ultrathin imprinting layer on MIPs provided fast mass transfer resulting in high adsorption capability for OA.

The selectivity of MIPs sensor was further evaluated using another two marine biotoxins, namely pectenotoxins-2 (PTX2) and gonyautoxin-2 (GTX2), as interferences (Fig.S4). The fluorescence enhancement of MIPs responding to OA was significantly higher than the other biotoxins, and those of NIPs. It can be reasonably explained that a large number of tridimensional cavities with tailor-made recognition sites have been created during the imprinting process to selectively and quickly match with OA. However, there were no binding sites generated on NIPs owing to the absence of dummy templates, and so not suitable to accommodate the target toxin. Hence, the MIPs sensor had high selectivity and sensitive fluorescent detection toward OA even in the presence of other interfering toxins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of MIPs sensor for OA
ce:section.ce:para: One of the prominent merits of the constructed ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor is the achievement of simple visual detection for OA with distinct changes of color tonality on the basis of the dual emission from the internal CDs and recognition signal APTES-NBD. In order to further evaluate the sensitive analysis and visual detection of the MIPs sensor for OA, a series of fluorescence experiments were carried out and the results are shown in Fig.4 . The fluorescence intensity of MIPs at 549 nm was regularly enhanced with the introduction of OA ranging of 0−1770 ng L−1 since the PET process was inhibited, but only an inapparent change from the reference signal was captured at around 667 nm (Fig. 4-A). Correspondingly, the distinct colour changes of the MIPs sensor from bright pink to yellow green could be observed by naked eyes in the insets. For only a single fluorescence signal sensor, it was difficult to directly distinguish visual detection of OA with a concolorous change as shown in the insets of Fig.4-C. Additional, the MIPs-based sensor also reflects a broad linear range between the fluorescence enhancement ratio and concentration variation from 100 to 1770 ng L-1, that was the fitting regression equation of (Fg/Fr)/(Fg/Fr)0 = 0.0018x+1.0082 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9984 in Fig. 4-B. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor on the basis of the establishment of internal target was more accurate than a single fluorescence signal source from SiO2@NBD@MIPs, which can eliminate the interference of other factors. For comparison, the fluorescence intensity of NIPs could also be improved by the addition of OA, however, the lower sensitivity and the narrower linear range was obtained (Fig. 4-D). Furthermore, the imprinting factor (IF) was employed to evaluate the imprinting effect of the imprinted material, that is defined as the ratio of slopes of the standard curves between MIPs and NIPs [20]. Obviously, MIPs and NIPs offered different linear relationships and a high imprinting factor could be calculated as 9. Wang et al. prepared a novel molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor via a sol–gel polymerization process for highly selective and sensitive detection of Phycocyanin, and a higher IF value of 9.1 was obtained [21].

Based on 3S/N (signal-to-noise), the limit of detection (LOD) for OA could reach 25 ng L−1. Additionally, the distinct bicolor changes can be observed in the OA concentration range of 644∼805 ng L−1, and thus it was suggested that the limit of visual detection is from 644 to 805 ng L−1. Therefore, the manufactured MIPs sensor possesses self-referencing ability and a promising potential for selective, sensitive and visual detection of OA.

High concentration of OA in seawater would imply recent or past HABs, and the identification and quantification of toxins in seawater performed on routine bases could serve as an early-warning parameter and provide a good indicator for shellfish toxicity. This will facilitate important decision-making process and minimize the risk of human exposure to toxins prior to the contamination of shellfish to a level above the legal limits [22]. Simple, fast and efficient analytical methods in seawater and sediment samples need to be developed to implement these regulation and surveillance of the toxins. For the purpose of demonstrating the practical application in real samples, the ratiometric MIPs sensor with a turn off-on fluorescence system was applied to detect OA in seawater and sediment samples. The result on the spiked and non-spiked samples was summarized in Table 1 for seawater and Table 2 for sediment. It can be seen that the recoveries of OA reached 90.0∼107.0 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 4.1 % (n = 3) for seawater and sediment. Besides, the comparison of the analytical performance between the ratiometric and non-ratiometric method was performed in No.3 seawater sample and the results show that the RSD values for ratiometric fluorescent method in four different spiked levels were in the range of 2.1∼4.1 % with an average of 3.1 %, while the RSD values for non-ratiometric method were from 3.3 % to 5.4 % with a higher average of 4.4 %. The results confirm that the prepared ratiometric fluorescence sensor is sufficiently efficient and sensitive for rapid, accurate and quantitative detection of OA in marine environmental samples, along with excellent visual effect.

Various analytical approaches have been established for OA detection, and the mouse bioassay (MBA) as the AOAC (Association of Official Analytical Chemists) official reference method is prohibited due to ethical arguments against the live animal assay. As an alternative, liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry detection (LC–MS) is a preferential choice for the trace analysis of OA in complex matrices. However, the detection method is primarily aimed for the quantification of toxins in shellfish tissue. At present, marine biotoxins represent a serious hazard due to bioaccumulation in shellfish and enter the food chain, so there is a growing interest on the analysis of algal toxins from environmental samples. The comparison between the developed MIPs sensor and other existing analytical methods for OA has been displayed in Table S1. To the best of our knowledge, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the application of OA detection is a first attempt in the seawater and sediment samples and the sensitivity of our detecting system is superior to most previously reported literatures. More importantly, its distinct color change from bright pink to yellow green is conducive to visual inspection, which doesn’t require expensive instrumentation or complex SPE process. Overall, the MIPs sensor has remarkable merits such as simplicity, good visualization, excellent sensitivity and enough reliability for practical utilization to determine OA toxin in marine environmental matrix.

In the study, a dual-emitting fluorescence MIPs nanosensor based on PET recognition principle has been ingeniously prepared and successfully applied in visual detection of OA in seawater and sediment samples. The green emission (APTES-NBD) with a strong fluorescence response is used as the detection signal, and the red emission (CDs) is employed as stable internal correction. The MIPs sensor has demonstrated high sensitivity for selective recognition and rapid analysis of OA with a good linear relationship, along with visual signal output observed by the naked eye. More excitingly, the simple, fast and sensitive nanosensor by a turn off-on switch system with the fluorescence enhancement reveals a prospective application for OA detection in seawater and sediment samples in the fields of environmental monitoring.

Ziru Lian: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Data curation, Resources. Min Zhao: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Jiangtao Wang: Data curation, Validation, Supervision. Ren-Cheng Yu: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. ZR2019MD026), Open Fund of CAS Key Laboratory of Marine Ecology and Environmental Sciences, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. KLMEES202003) and the Joint Funds of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. U1806202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130465.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ocean warming since 1982 has expanded the niche of toxic algal blooms in the North Atlantic and North Pacific oceans
PNAS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. J. Gobler, O. M. Doherty, T. K. Hattenrath-Lehmann, A. W. Griffith, Y. Kang, R. W. Litaker, Ocean warming since 1982 has expanded the niche of toxic algal blooms in the North Atlantic and North Pacific oceans, PNAS 114 (2017) 4975-4980.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sorption of okadaic acid lipophilic toxin onto plastics in seawater
Mar. Pollut. Bull.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.T. Costa, A. Rudnitskaya, C. Vale, L. Guilhermino, M.J. Botelho, Sorption of okadaic acid lipophilic toxin onto plastics in seawater, Mar. Pollut. Bull. 157 (2020) 111322-111331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assessing the presence of marine toxins in bivalve molluscs from southwest India
Toxicon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.D.Turner, M. Dhanji-Rapkova, S. Rowland-Pilgrim, L. M. Turner, A. Rai, M. N. Venugopal, I. Karunasagar, A. Godhe, Assessing the presence of marine toxins in bivalve molluscs from southwest India, Toxicon 140 (2017) 147-156.
